focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

By ten the next morning, the courthouse steps hissed and hammered, a boiler valve blown open. Command had burned her phone since dawn and her mother had left nine seconds of silence on voicemail, and none of it changed where morning landed. Chant rose against chant, whistles against bullhorns, and somewhere in the middle somebody had put a snare drum on a folding chair and kept beating the same roll until it turned into a headache for half the block.

Lena stood inside the metal barricade corridor with sweat running down her spine under her vest and counted bodies the way she counted shell casings: fast, again, then one more time because the first count was never enough. Fifty-two on the west side by the veterans memorial. Forty-eight on the east side under the dead magnolia. Five deputies between them and two city uniforms who looked nineteen and scared of their own batons.

The sun sat straight overhead, white and blunt. Heat came off the asphalt in visible waves, and the paint on the bike-lane stripes smelled sweet and chemical, melted-plastic sweet. Cicadas screamed from the oaks beyond the jail annex and never once took a breath.

"We're about one shove from a pileup," Beau said beside her. He had taken off his tie and stuffed it in his back pocket, where it hung in a wilted knot, pure surrender. "I hate to be old and prophetic, but this is how every bad shrimp boil starts. Too many cousins, not enough shade."

"Keep west side six feet off the rails," Lena said. She kept her voice flat and procedural because it helped her think. "No chemical spray unless I call it. If they surge, we split at the fountain, not at the stairs."

"Copy." He scratched the edge of his jaw and glanced at the crowd. "You ate anything?"

"Coffee."

"That ain't food, partner."...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...tly like Mara said. Tina sat behind the wheel with both hands wrapped around a gas-station coffee cup she had not touched.

Lena approached slow, badge visible, empty hands. "I came alone."

Tina unlocked the passenger door but did not let go of the lock button. "You write this down right. You don't let them make him sound stupid."

"I write what happened."

Tina passed over a cheap black flip phone wrapped in two Burger Barn napkins. "Found it under his seat when they let me pull his jacket from the impound lot. He used it when he didn't want calls on his real phone."

Lena set the consent form on the center console and read every line out loud before Tina signed. Then she opened the flip phone and typed the birthday Tina whispered, one digit at a time. The inbox held three saved voice memos under Lyle's own number.

The last one was time-stamped 10:47 p.m.

Lyle's voice came through tinny and wind-cut, trying for swagger and not making it. "Note to self. Deputy's office called from blocked. Said meet at Cormorant before daylight with permit folder and don't bring nobody. Said we could clear code-enforcement heat if I play nice. If this goes bad, Tina, don't let 'em say I wandered down there drunk."

Lena replayed it and photographed the call log. A blocked incoming number had hit that same phone at 02:12 a.m., then again at 02:18, inside the same twenty-minute window as the masked morgue override.

She copied the memo to her recorder, sealed the flip phone in an evidence bag, and wrote chain start in Tina's presence with time, date, and witness signature.

When thunder cracked over the lot, Lena already had the lead in her hand, on paper, before command could rename it, and she knew the next days would be a fight to keep that timestamp from getting filed into silence.
